perturbations.original:
\begin{proof} Any two polynomials in $\mathcal I$ of minimal degree in $S_n$ differ only by factors in $\K(n)$: if $A$ and $B$ are two such polynomials and $a,b$ are their leading coefficients, then $bA-aB\in\mathcal I$ has smaller degree in $S_n$ and must therefore be 0. The leading coefficients of the polynomials of minimal degree in $\mathcal I$ form an ideal in $\K[n]_{\Za}$. Since this ring is principal~%
\cite{Samuel1971}, this
ideal is generated by one element. Up to multiplication by an
invertible element of $\K[n]_{\Za}$ (a fraction with
numerator and denominator in $\Za$), it can be made
polynomial, of minimal degree and with leading coefficient~1.
\end{proof}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{proof} Any two polynomials in $\mathcal I$ of minimal degree in $S_n$ differ only by factors in $\K(n)$: if $A$ and $B$ are two such polynomials and $a,b$ are their leading coefficients, then $bA-aB\in\mathcal I$ has smaller degree in $S_n$ and must therefore be 0. This implies that the minimal element of $\mathcal I$ exists and is unique. Since the minimal element is unique, the leading coefficients of the polynomials of minimal degree in $\mathcal I$ form an ideal in $\K[n]_{\Za}$ generated by one element. Up to multiplication by an invertible element of $\K[n]_{\Za}$, it can be made polynomial, of minimal degree and with leading coefficient~1.
\end{proof}
perturbations.why_wrong: The proof uses the existence and uniqueness of the minimal element (the statement of the Lemma) to justify why the ideal of leading coefficients is principal, which is the core step needed to prove the Lemma itself.

perturbations.original:
\begin{proof}
The left-coprimality of $U,V$ is a property of lclms in $\Sh$.

  By design, $d\lclm_\Sh (A,B)=(dU)A=(dU)B$ is a common left multiple
  of $A$ and $B$ in $\RecI$. It has minimal degree in $S_n$, since
  $\lclm_\Sh$ does in~$\Sh$. 
This implies that there exists $\delta\in\K[n]$ such that
$\mclm_\RecI(A,B)=\delta\lclm_\Sh(A,B)$. Right division by $A$ shows that $\delta U\in\RecI$. Similarly, $\delta V\in\RecI$. This implies that $\delta$ is a multiple of $d$ and therefore is equal to $d$ by minimality.
\end{proof}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{proof}
The left-coprimality of $U,V$ is a property of lclms in $\Sh$.

  By design, $d\lclm_\Sh (A,B)=(dU)A=(dV)B$ is a common left multiple
  of $A$ and $B$ in $\RecI$. It has minimal degree in $S_n$, since
  $\lclm_\Sh$ does in~$\Sh$. 
This implies that there exists $\delta\in\K[n]$ such that
$\mclm_\RecI(A,B)=\delta\lclm_\Sh(A,B)$. Right division by $A$ shows that $\delta U\in\RecI$. Similarly, $\delta V\in\RecI$. Since $d$ is the least common multiple of the denominators of $U$ and $V$, and $\delta U, \delta V$ are in $\RecI$, it follows that $d$ must be a multiple of $\delta$, and therefore $\delta=d$ by minimality.
\end{proof}
perturbations.why_wrong: The logic is reversed: if $\delta U$ and $\delta V$ are polynomials (in $\RecI$), then $\delta$ must be a multiple of the least common multiple of the denominators of $U$ and $V$ (which is $d$), not the other way around.

perturbations.original:
\begin{proof}
The proof is effective. Let $\tilde U$ and $\tilde V$ be such that
$A=G\tilde U$ and $B=G\tilde V$. Let $g(n)$ and $u(n)$ be minimal
elements of $\K[n]_\Za$ (for the degree of the part that vanishes
only at
{elements of $\mathbb N$}) such that $g(n)G$,
$U:=u(n)\tilde U$ and $V:=u
(n)\tilde V$ all belong to $\RecI$. Define $\hat G$ and $d(n)$ by 
\begin{equation}\label{eq:commutewithG}
\lclm_\RecI(g(n)G,u(n))=\hat G u(n)=d(n)g(n)G
\end{equation}
and let $\delta(n)=d(n)g(n)$. Then,
$\delta(n)A=\delta(n)G\tilde U=\hat Gu(n)\tilde U=\hat GU$
and similarly for $B$. Finally, 
\[
  \gcld_\Sh(U,V)=\gcld_\Sh(u(n)\tilde U,u(n)\tilde V)=\gcld_\Sh
  (\tilde U,\tilde V)
\]
and the last $\gcld_\Sh$ has degree~0 by maximality of~$\gcld_\Sh
(A,B)$.
\end{proof}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{proof}
The proof is effective. Let $\tilde U$ and $\tilde V$ be such that
$A=G\tilde U$ and $B=G\tilde V$. Let $g(n)$ and $u(n)$ be minimal
elements of $\K[n]_\Za$ such that $g(n)G$,
$U:=u(n)\tilde U$ and $V:=u
(n)\tilde V$ all belong to $\RecI$. Define $\hat G$ and $d(n)$ by 
\begin{equation}\label{eq:commutewithG}
\lclm_\RecI(g(n)G,u(n))=\hat G u(n)=d(n)g(n)G
\end{equation}
and let $\delta(n)=d(n)g(n)$. Then,
$\delta(n)A=\delta(n)G\tilde U=\hat Gu(n)\tilde U=\hat GU$
and similarly for $B$. Finally, 
\[
  \gcld_\Sh(U,V)=\gcld_\Sh(u(n)\tilde U,u(n)\tilde V)=u(n)\gcld_\Sh
  (\tilde U,\tilde V)
\]
and since $u(n)$ is a polynomial, the last $\gcld_\Sh$ has degree~0.
\end{proof}
perturbations.why_wrong: The operator $\gcld_\Sh$ is defined over the shift algebra $\Sh = \K(n)[S_n]$. In this algebra, $u(n)$ is an invertible element (a scalar), so $\gcld_\Sh(u(n)\tilde U, u(n)\tilde V)$ is equal to $\gcld_\Sh(\tilde U, \tilde V)$, not $u(n)\gcld_\Sh(\tilde U, \tilde V)$ in a way that affects the degree in $S_n$.

perturbations.original:
\begin{proof} The proof reduces to modules over the ring $\Sh$,
whose left-principal character is then exploited. 
Let $\tilde{M}$ be the left-$\Sh$-module generated
by
$M$. 

1. Properness of $M$ implies
that $ \tilde{M} \cap(\Sh\times\{0\})=\{
(0,0)\}$: The elements of~$\tilde M$ are
finite linear
combinations~$C=a_1
(n_1,d_1)+\dots+a_k (n_k,d_k)$
of elements of $M$ with coefficients~$a_i$ in~$\Sh$ rather than
$\Rec$. If such a
combination has a~0 denominator,
then, multiplying~$C$ by a common multiple of the denominators
of the~$a_i$ gives
an element of~$M$. This element is proper, which implies
that its numerator
is~0 and thus also that of~$C$.

2. $\tilde M$ has a generating pair: The projection of
$\tilde M$ on the second
coordinate is a nonzero
left ideal 
of $\Sh$. Let $y\in\Sh$~be a generator of it and~$\tilde G=(x,y)$ a
corresponding element of~$\tilde M$. For any nonzero element~$
P=(u,v)\in\tilde M$, there exists $a\in\Sh$ such that $v=ay$ and then
$P-a\tilde G=(u-ax,0)=(0,0)$ (by properness) implies that $P=a\tilde
G$. Clearing
denominators gives~$c\in\Za$ such that~$G:=c
\tilde G\in M$.

3. The pair $G$ generates~$M$: For any $A \in {M}$, there exists
$h \in \Sh$,  such that $A = h\tilde G=hc^{-1}G$.
Multiplying~$hc^{-1}$ by an appropriate polynomial~$a$ so that
$H=ahc^{-1}\in\RecI$ concludes.
\end{proof}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{proof} The proof reduces to modules over the ring $\Sh$,
whose left-principal character is then exploited. 
Let $\tilde{M}$ be the left-$\Sh$-module generated
by
$M$. 

1. $\tilde M$ has a generating pair: The projection of
$\tilde M$ on the second
coordinate is a nonzero
left ideal 
of $\Sh$. Let $y\in\Sh$~be a generator of it and~$\tilde G=(x,y)$ a
corresponding element of~$\tilde M$. For any nonzero element~$
P=(u,v)\in\tilde M$, there exists $a\in\Sh$ such that $v=ay$ and then
$P-a\tilde G=(u-ax,0)=(0,0)$ implies that $P=a\tilde
G$. Clearing
denominators gives~$c\in\Za$ such that~$G:=c
\tilde G\in M$.

2. The pair $G$ generates~$M$: For any $A \in {M}$, there exists
$h \in \Sh$,  such that $A = h\tilde G=hc^{-1}G$.
Multiplying~$hc^{-1}$ by an appropriate polynomial~$a$ so that
$H=ahc^{-1}\in\RecI$ concludes.
\end{proof}
perturbations.why_wrong: The proof omits the crucial step of proving the 'Properness' of the module $\tilde{M}$. Without showing that $\tilde{M} \cap (\Sh \times \{0\}) = \{(0,0)\}$, the implication $(u-ax, 0) = (0,0)$ in step 1 is unjustified.

perturbations.original:
\begin{proof}
Let~$A,B$ be two nonzero fractions in~$M$ and $G$ a quasi-generating
fraction of~$M$. Then there exists $a,b$ in~$\K[n]\setminus\{0\}$ and
$H_A,H_B$ in~$\Rec$ such that
\[aA=H_AG,\quad bB=H_BG.\]
Let~$UH_A=VH_B$ be a common left multiple of~$H_A,H_B$ in~$\Rec$.
Then~$UaA=VbB$, proving the $\RecI$-equivalence of~$A,B$.
\end{proof}
perturbations.why_wrong: The proof assumes the existence of $U, V$ such that $UA=VB$ (which is exactly what $\RecI$-equivalence means) as a property of the module, instead of deriving it from the existence of a quasi-generating fraction $G$ as established in the previous proposition.

perturbations.original:
\begin{proof}
This is a direct corollary of \zcref{lemma:quasi-gen-pair}. If $
(p,q)$ is irreducible and a multiple of a quasi-generating
fraction~$G$,
then~$G$ itself has to be irreducible and therefore~$(p,q)=c(n)G$ for
some~$c(n)\in\K(n)$. This makes~$(p,q)$ a quasi-generating fraction
of~$M$. Also, all other quasi-generating fractions are multiples of
that same~$G$ by some element of~$\K(n)$, which makes them
irreducible.
\end{proof}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{proof}
This is a direct corollary of \zcref{lemma:quasi-gen-pair}. If $
(p,q)$ is irreducible and a multiple of a quasi-generating
fraction~$G$,
then~$G$ itself must be reducible because $(p,q)$ is the simplest form in the module. Therefore~$(p,q)=c(n)G$ for
some~$c(n)\in\K(n)$. This makes~$(p,q)$ a quasi-generating fraction
of~$M$. Also, all other quasi-generating fractions are multiples of
that same~$G$ by some element of~$\K(n)$, which makes them
irreducible.
\end{proof}
perturbations.why_wrong: The claim that $G$ must be reducible because $(p,q)$ is irreducible is logically flawed. If $(p,q)$ is irreducible and $(p,q) = c(n)G$, then $G$ must also be irreducible (up to a scalar in $\K(n)$), otherwise $(p,q)$ would be reducible.

perturbations.original:
\begin{proof}[Proof that the operations do not depend on the
equivalence class for $\equivZA$.]
Let $a,b$ be in $\RecI^\times$ and $p,q$ in $\RecI$. We first relate the
cofactors of $ap$ and $bq$ to those of $p,q$. First, since $\lclm$s
are taken monic with respect to $S_n$, 
we have
\[
  \lclm_\Sh \left (p, q\right) = \lclm_\Sh \left (ap,bq\right).
\]
\zcref{prop:mclm-rec} then implies
\[
  \mclm_\RecI \left (p, q\right) = \mclm_\RecI \left (ap,bq\right).
\]
In terms of cofactors, the equalities
\[
  (q)^{p} p =  (p)^{q} q = \mclm_\RecI\left (p, q\right)=
(q)^{p}\frac1a a p =  (p)^{q}\frac 1b b q
\]
give the following cofactors in $\RecI$:
\[
  (bq)^{ap}=(q)^p\frac1a,\quad 
  (ap)^{bq}=(p)^q\frac 1b.
\]
Therefore,
\begin{gather*}
(bq_2)^{ap_2}ap_1+(ap_2)^{bq_2}bq_1=
(q_2)^{p_2}\frac1a ap_1+(p_2)^{q_2}\frac1bbp_2=
(q_2)^{p_2}p_1+(p_2)^{q_2}q_1,\\
((ap_1)^{bq_2}bq_1,(bq_2)^{ap_1}ap_2)=
(p_1)^{q_2}\frac1bbq_1,(q_2)^{p_1}\frac1aap_2)=
((p_1)^{q_2}q_1,(q_2)^{p_1}p_2)
\end{gather*}
show the result for $\oplus$ and for $\otimes$.
\end{proof}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{proof}[Proof that the operations do not depend on the
equivalence class for $\equivZA$.]
Let $a,b$ be in $\RecI^\times$ and $p,q$ in $\RecI$. We first relate the
cofactors of $ap$ and $bq$ to those of $p,q$. First, since $\lclm$s
are taken monic with respect to $S_n$, 
we have
\[
  \lclm_\Sh \left (p, q\right) = \lclm_\Sh \left (ap,bq\right).
\]
\zcref{prop:mclm-rec} then implies
\[
  \mclm_\RecI \left (p, q\right) = a b \mclm_\RecI \left (p,q\right).
\]
In terms of cofactors, the equalities
\[
  (q)^{p} p =  (p)^{q} q = \mclm_\RecI\left (p, q\right)
\]
give the result for $\oplus$ and for $\otimes$.
\end{proof}
perturbations.why_wrong: The implication that $\mclm_\RecI(ap, bq) = ab \mclm_\RecI(p, q)$ is incorrect. Since $a, b \in \RecI^\times$ (scalars in $\K[n]$), they do not change the $\lclm$ in $\Sh$, and by Proposition 3.2, the $\mclm$ in $\RecI$ only changes by a scalar factor $d$ related to denominators, not by the product $ab$.

perturbations.original:
\begin{proof} Take $(p_1,p_2)\in\Ann_\FrecI(u,-v)$ and $(q_1,q_2)\in\Ann_\FrecI (u,-w)$. Then the matrix
\[
N=\begin{pmatrix}p_1&-p_2&0\\ q_1&0&-q_2\end{pmatrix}
\]
is such that $N\cdot(u,v,w)^T=0$. Multiplying the first row by $(q_2)^
{p_2}$, the second one by $(p_2)^{q_2}$ and adding gives the first
inclusion. For the second one, take $(p_1,p_2)\in\Ann_\FrecI(v,-w)$ and
$(q_1,q_2)\in\Ann_\FrecI(u,-v)$. The matrix
\[
M=\begin{pmatrix}0&p_1&-p_2\\q_1&-q_2&0\end{pmatrix},
\]
is such that $M\cdot(u,v,w)^T=0$. Multiplying the first row by $(q_2)^{p_1}$, the second one by $(p_1)^{q_2}$ and adding concludes.
\end{proof}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{proof} Take $(p_1,p_2)\in\Ann_\FrecI(u,-v)$ and $(q_1,q_2)\in\Ann_\FrecI (u,-w)$. Then the matrix
\[
N=\begin{pmatrix}p_1&-p_2&0\\ q_1&0&-q_2\end{pmatrix}
\]
is such that $N\cdot(u,v,w)^T=0$. Multiplying the first row by $q_2$, the second one by $p_2$ and adding gives $(q_2 p_1 + p_2 q_1, p_2 q_2) \in \Ann_\FrecI(u, -(v+w))$. For the second one, take $(p_1,p_2)\in\Ann_\FrecI(v,-w)$ and
$(q_1,q_2)\in\Ann_\FrecI(u,-v)$. The matrix
\[
M=\begin{pmatrix}0&p_1&-p_2\\q_1&-q_2&0\end{pmatrix},
\]
is such that $M\cdot(u,v,w)^T=0$. Multiplying the first row by $q_2$, the second one by $p_1$ and adding concludes.
\end{proof}
perturbations.why_wrong: The proof uses commutative multiplication (multiplying by $q_2$ and $p_2$) instead of the non-commutative cofactors $(q_2)^{p_2}$ and $(p_2)^{q_2}$ required in the Ore algebra $\RecI$. In $\RecI$, $q_2 p_1 + p_2 q_1$ does not correctly annihilate $u$ when $p_2$ and $q_2$ do not commute with the other operators.

perturbations.original:
\begin{proof} The proof is by induction. For any $\lambda\in\K$ and
any sequence $u$, one has $(\lambda,1)\in\Ann_\FrecI(u,-\lambda u)$. If
$(f_n)$ denotes the sequence of coefficients of $F$, then the
coefficient of $x^n$ in $xF$ is $f_{n-1}$, thus they are related by $
[x^n](F)=S_n[x^n](xF)$ or equivalently $(1,S_n)\in\Ann_\FrecI([x^n]
(F),-[x^n](xF))$. Similarly, the coefficient of $x^n$ in $F'$ is $(n+1)f_
{n+1}$, which proves the case of $\partial$. If $t=t_1+t_2$, let
$L_i=\mathfrak D_\delta(t_i)$ for $i=1, 2$. Then we have $L=L_1+L_2$ and by
induction, for any $F$, $\mathfrak R_{\mathrm{Taylor}}(t_i)\in\Ann_\FrecI([x^n]
(F),-[x^n](L_i(F)))$ for $i=1,2$ so that the result follows by 
\zcref{prop:annihilators}. Finally, if $t=t_1\times t_2$, then with
$L_i$ as above, $L=L_1L_2$ and by induction, for any power series
$F,G$, $\mathfrak R_{\mathrm{Taylor}}(t_1)\in\Ann_\Frec([x^n](G),-[x^n](L_1
(G)))$ and $\mathfrak R_{\mathrm{Taylor}}(t_2)\in\Ann_\FrecI([x^n](F),-[x^n](L_2
(F)))$. With $G=L_2(F)$, \zcref{prop:annihilators} concludes.
\end{proof}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{proof} The proof is by induction. For any $\lambda\in\K$ and
any sequence $u$, one has $(1,\lambda)\in\Ann_\FrecI(u,-\lambda u)$. If
$(f_n)$ denotes the sequence of coefficients of $F$, then the
coefficient of $x^n$ in $xF$ is $f_{n-1}$, thus they are related by $
[x^n](F)=S_n[x^n](xF)$ or equivalently $(1,S_n)\in\Ann_\FrecI([x^n]
(F),-[x^n](xF))$. Similarly, the coefficient of $x^n$ in $F'$ is $(n+1)f_
{n+1}$, which proves the case of $\partial$. If $t=t_1+t_2$, let
$L_i=\mathfrak D_\delta(t_i)$ for $i=1, 2$. Then we have $L=L_1+L_2$ and by
induction, for any $F$, $\mathfrak R_{\mathrm{Taylor}}(t_i)\in\Ann_\FrecI([x^n]
(F),-[x^n](L_i(F)))$ for $i=1,2$ so that the result follows by 
\zcref{prop:annihilators}. Finally, if $t=t_1\times t_2$, then with
$L_i$ as above, $L=L_1L_2$ and by induction, for any power series
$F,G$, $\mathfrak R_{\mathrm{Taylor}}(t_1)\in\Ann_\Frec([x^n](G),-[x^n](L_1
(G)))$ and $\mathfrak R_{\mathrm{Taylor}}(t_2)\in\Ann_\FrecI([x^n](F),-[x^n](L_2
(F)))$. With $G=L_2(F)$, \zcref{prop:annihilators} concludes.
\end{proof}
perturbations.why_wrong: The base case for a constant $\lambda$ is incorrect. If $(p, q) \in \Ann(u, -v)$, then $pu - qv = 0$. For $v = \lambda u$, we need $pu - q(\lambda u) = 0$, which means $p = q\lambda$. For $(1, \lambda)$, this would mean $1 = \lambda \cdot \lambda$, which is false. The correct pair is $(\lambda, 1)$ as $\lambda u - 1(\lambda u) = 0$.

perturbations.original:
\begin{proof} Part of the proof is due to Lewanowicz~%
\cite{Lewanowicz1995}. We give it here for the sake of
completeness. {The existence of the sequence  $([\psi_n](xf))_{n \in
\nat}$ is a consequence of the fact that, for all $n\geq 0$, $x \psi_n
(x)$ is a finite linear combination of $\psi_{n+1}, \psi_n$ and $\psi_
{n-1}$, by the 3-term recurrence~\eqref{eq:3term-rec}, and the
existence of $ ([\psi_n](f))_{n \in \nat}$}.
The proof reduces to 
\begin{align*}
 h_{n+1} \Xden \cdot [\psi_n](xf)  & =  \langle  x f | \psi_{n+1} \rangle_w =  \langle   f |  x  \psi_{n+1} (x) \rangle_w  \\
 &  =   \langle  f |   (a(n) S_n^2+b(n) S_n+c(n)) \cdot \psi_n(x)
 \rangle_w \\ 
  & =  (a(n) S_n^2+b(n) S_n+c(n))   \cdot \langle  f |  \psi_n(x) \rangle_w \\
  & = h_{n+1}  \Xnum \cdot [\psi_n](f).\qedhere
\end{align*}
\end{proof}
perturbations.why_wrong: The index in the normalization constant $h_{n+1}$ is changed to $h_n$. According to the 3-term recurrence relation (Eq 4.1), $x\psi_n$ involves $\psi_{n+1}$, and the inner product $\langle xf, \psi_{n+1} \rangle$ is what relates to $h_{n+1}$ in the standard theory of orthogonal polynomials.
